text: The video displays the Einstein Vision version one system, featuring a robotic arm and a camera head weighing approximately two kilograms.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic surgery using a 10mm trocar in the operating theater. The video appears blurred due to the dual vision system, and the surgical team is wearing spectacles for the 3D laparoscopy.

The surgeon examines the area where the cyst is attached to the uterus, preparing to break the cyst wall for aspiration. The right fallopian tube is visibly adherent to the ovary, and the surgeon works to release this adhesion while separating two cysts.
Using a 3D laparoscopic view, the surgeon releases the right ovary from the pelvic side, carefully separating it from surrounding adhesions.

The surgeon aspirates the cyst on the left side, revealing a thick, dried chocolate cyst. The ovary is released from the pelvic side wall, and the adhesions between the ovaries and the uterus are minimal. The surgeon tests for tubal patency, confirming that both the right and left fallopian tubes are patent.

The surgeon examines the anterior aspect of the surgical field, revealing the bladder adherent to the uterus with multiple nodules present in the area, indicating extensive adhesions requiring further dissection.
The surgeon uses a non-absorbable suture on a straight needle to hook up the ovaries, leaving the cyst intact. The surgical focus shifts to working in the pouch of Douglas.

The surgeon encounters the rectum adhered to surrounding tissue, highlighting the complexity of the dissection area. The use of three-D laparoscopy provides enhanced visualization of the anatomical structures, aiding in precise separation without damaging the rectum.

The surgeon navigates the right pararectal space, identifying the adhesion between the rectum and vagina. Using three-dimensional laparoscopy, the surgeon seeks the demarcation line to carefully separate the rectum from the vaginal tissue, noting minimal adhesion on the left side.

The surgeon identifies the demarcation line between the rectum and uterus and begins to carefully shave the nodular adhesions from the uterus, aiming to detach the rectum without leaving any nodules on it.

The surgeon carefully releases the rectum from its dense adhesion to the uterus, gradually separating the two structures. Once the rectum is freed, it descends, allowing the surgeon to continue with the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon locates the right ureter and isolates it, preparing to excise diseased tissue and nodules from the uterus to reduce recurrence rates.
The surgeon uses a bisect instrument to coagulate and cut nodules on the right utero sacral ligament, carefully excising the diseased tissue.
The surgeon palpates the nodules on the uterus, preparing to excise them while carefully preserving normal tissue, demonstrating the technique of endometriotic surgery.

The surgeon palpates the tissue to identify hard nodules, selectively excising only the diseased tissue while ensuring the rectum has been safely dissected away, utilizing the advantages of three-D laparoscopy.

The surgeon uses a bisect instrument to simultaneously coagulate and cut tissue during the excision of endometriotic nodules, enhancing efficiency by eliminating the need to switch between separate coagulation and cutting tools.

The surgeon examines the ureter and palpates the area, identifying additional endometriotic nodules. Using a bisect instrument, the surgeon safely coagulates and cuts the nodules, aided by the depth perception provided by 3D laparoscopy.

The surgeon uses a three-dimensional laparoscopic approach to excise endometriotic disease from the peritoneum above the ureter, carefully dissecting and removing the affected tissue while preserving vital structures.
The surgeon tests the integrity of the rectum after excising endometriotic tissue, ensuring that vital structures such as the ovaries, ureters, and bladder remain unharmed.

The surgeon performs a Michelin test by placing water in the pelvis and injecting air and methylene blue to identify a serosal tear on the rectum. The serosal areas are brought together for suturing to repair the tear.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 polyglecting suture to close a slight raw area, utilizing the enhanced depth perception provided by 3-D laparoscopy for precise suturing.

The surgeon begins the cystectomy, carefully separating the cyst wall from the ovary. Vasopressin is injected between the cyst wall and the ovary to facilitate the dissection, ensuring minimal removal of the cyst wall while avoiding leaving any remnants.

The surgeon uses hydrodissection to separate the cyst wall from the ovary, ensuring minimal bleeding and preserving the ovary for future fertility. The three-dimensional laparoscopic view enhances visibility of the anatomical structures during this critical step.

Using a three-dimensional laparoscopic view, the surgeon completes the cystectomy on the left ovary, ensuring maximum preservation of ovarian tissue. Any remaining cyst tissue is carefully removed before proceeding to the right side.

The surgeon injects vasopressin into the space between the cyst wall and the ovary, visibly separating the cyst wall. The cyst wall is then carefully teased away from the ovary using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection techniques, ensuring minimal impact on the ovarian reserve due to bilateral endometrioma.

The surgeon carefully approaches the ovarian hilum during the laparoscopic cystectomy, using electrocautery to precisely excise the cyst wall while preserving ovarian tissue. The three-dimensional laparoscope provides enhanced visualization for precise tissue access.

The surgeon has completed the cystectomy and is now focusing on the anterior wall of the uterus. Visible endometriotic nodules are present on the left utero sacral ligament and the bladder. The surgeon prepares to remove the endometriotic tissue while carefully avoiding injury to the bladder.
The surgeon uses scissors to carefully excise an endometriotic nodule from the bladder, minimizing thermal damage to surrounding tissues.

The surgeon excises endometriotic tissue from the round ligament, using precise techniques to remove diseased tissue while preserving normal structures. The surgical field is viewed in two-dimensional imaging, though the surgeon notes the enhanced clarity provided by three-dimensional imaging.

The surgeon uses a bisect to gently excise endometriotic tissue from the left side, completing the removal of disease in that area. The procedure then progresses to the right side, where endometriotic tissue is visible on the right utero-sacral ligament and involves the bladder.

The surgeon carefully dissects and excises endometriotic nodules, using a bisect tool away from the bladder and switching to scissors for areas closer to the bladder to avoid thermal damage. The focus is on removing as much diseased tissue as possible while preserving surrounding structures.

The surgeon excises and removes visible endometriotic disease near the bladder, ensuring thorough removal to minimize recurrence and improve fertility outcomes. Despite removing a significant amount of disease, the surgeon identifies additional areas requiring further excision.
The surgeon uses a sharp incision technique to excise the endometriotic disease, employing a grasper to gently pull on the affected tissue for precise removal.
The surgeon identifies a small fibroid in the surgical field and proceeds to enucleate and remove it. Following the removal, the surgeon repairs the defect left by the excised fibroid.
The surgeon uses a polyglycine one suture to repair the defect, employing a baseball suture technique to invert and close the shallow lesion completely.

The surgeon completes the closure of the defect with a single layer suture. The fibroid and nodules have been removed, and the next step involves repairing the ovarian defect. The ovary is visibly repaired to prevent the defect from remaining open.

The surgeon performs an ovariopexy by hooking the ovary to the peritoneum using a polyglycine 2-0 suture, leaving a visible length of two to three centimeters. The right ovary is already secured, and the surgeon proceeds to hitch up the left ovary after repairing it.

The surgeon identifies and excises a nodule using a bisect tool, followed by another nodule located on top of the right ureter, which is carefully excised using sharp scissors to avoid diathermy. The surgical field is thoroughly examined to remove visible disease nodules, enhancing the patient's chances of spontaneous pregnancy.

The surgeon places anti-adhesion barriers throughout the pelvic region to prevent postoperative adhesions. The uterus is visible without the uterine manipulator and remains in a non-retroverted position, demonstrating the final appearance of the pelvis at the end of the surgery.
The surgeon addresses uterovaginal prolapse by using a hook and suction instrument to open the peritoneum over the sacral promontory.

The surgeon opens the posterior part of the uterus and identifies the right utero sacral ligament. A space is created to separate the rectum from the peritoneum, with dissection proceeding along the right utero sacral ligament.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum to access the levator ani muscle, preparing to place a posterior mesh for prolapse support.
The surgeon uses a 3D laparoscopic approach to open the peritoneum on the right side, revealing the right utero sacral ligament.

The surgeon makes a U-shaped incision posterior to the uterus, pushing the rectum downward. The rectum is visible in the center, and the surgeon clears the overlying fat tissue to access the levator ani on either side.
The surgeon navigates the left lateral pararectal space, dissecting down to the levator ani using three-dimensional laparoscopy, which enhances depth perception during the procedure.
The surgeon performs a slow dissection near the medial rectal artery, carefully avoiding injury to the vessel while progressing towards the levator ani.

The surgeon dissects on the right side, with the rectum visible in the center of the surgical field. The right medial pararectal space is opened, and dissection continues towards the levator ani muscle.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the right puborectalis muscle, preparing to stitch the mesh to it. The levator ani muscle is exposed and clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a subtotal hysterectomy, removing the body of the uterus while leaving the cervix intact. The cervix is prepared as a site for mesh attachment during sacrocopopexy.

The surgeon has completed the subtotal hysterectomy and is now releasing surrounding fat tissue to expose the rectum. The rectum is clearly visible, and the surgeon prepares to place mesh on top for posterior support.
The surgeon excises surrounding tissues and places the first stitch into the left puborectalis muscle, preparing to attach the stitch to the mesh for posterior support.
The surgeon uses a non-absorbable suture to attach the mesh deep within the surgical field, where instruments have limited reach.
The surgeon ties down the non-absorbable suture, securing the mesh in place with seven or eight knots. Stitches are being placed on both the right and left sides.
The surgeon places a stitch into the right puborectalis muscle, pulling the needle out before attaching the mesh to the muscle.

The surgeon attaches the mesh to the rectum using multiple throws of non-absorbable suture, ensuring the mesh sits securely for posterior support. The mesh is visibly positioned on the rectum, providing structural reinforcement.
The surgeon dissects the bladder down to the trigone, aiming to correct any cystocele. The depth of dissection is visually enhanced by the 3D imaging, allowing precise attachment of the mesh.
The surgeon places a fine 2-0 non-absorbable suture to attach the mesh to the lower area, ensuring it is positioned to correct the cystocele.
The surgeon places two additional stitches on the lateral side to attach the mesh to the vaginal wall, ensuring it is positioned superficially at the level of the vagina.
The surgeon positions the mesh below the bladder and sutures it in place, ensuring it sits securely to provide support.
The surgeon places a large number two stitch into the longitudinal ligament of the sacral promontory, followed by another stitch, securing the mesh to the ligament.
The surgeon completes the sacral colpopexy by tying and fixing the mesh to the sacral promontory using a suture.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and the already cut left uterine artery. Using harmonic scalpel, the surgeon carefully de-roofs the ureteric tunnel, ensuring the ureter remains uninjured.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to de-roof the ureteric tunnel, carefully dissecting around the ascending branch of the uterine artery.
The surgeon uses the ERBI BiClan, a precise coagulative instrument, to perform tissue coagulation. This smart bipolar device is reusable and offers controlled coagulation during the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the knuckle of the ureter and begins releasing surrounding tissue using a precise bipolar coagulative instrument, ensuring careful manipulation to avoid injury.
The surgeon uses the ERBI BiClan bipolar instrument to coagulate and cut tissue near the ureter, avoiding the use of harmonics or bisect tools in this area to prevent injury to the ureter.

The surgeon uses a bipolar instrument to coagulate and cut tissue around the ureter, releasing it from surrounding structures. The three-dimensional laparoscopic view enhances precision in this delicate area.
The surgeon identifies the Yabuki space, which is medial to the ureter, and completes the dissection of the ureter during a radical hysterectomy.
The surgeon releases the ureter from surrounding bladder tissue, using harmonic instruments to separate the bladder area.
The surgeon has completely de-roofed the ureter, exposing it fully within the surgical field.
The surgeon approaches the right side of the surgical field, noting the increased difficulty due to the angle of approach, with all instruments positioned on the left side.
The surgeon approaches the left side to de-roof the ureter using harmonic scalpel, carefully navigating through the tunnel to release the ureter from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon releases the parametrial tissue on top of the ureter, which will be removed with the uterus. The surgeon continues to de-roof the ureter on the right side using surgical instruments.

The surgeon highlights the ureter, focusing on an area known as the 'knuckle,' where the ureter shifts and turns. This region is visually identified as one of the most dangerous areas during the procedure.
The surgeon isolates the ureter, carefully dissecting the surrounding tissue to prevent accidental cutting or coagulation of the ureter.
The surgeon identifies the medial part of the ureter within the Yabuki space, observing the ureter's path through this anatomical area.
The surgeon uses precise dissection and coagulation techniques to release surrounding tissues from the ureter, utilizing the enhanced visualization provided by 3D laparoscopy.

The surgeon uses scissors to release tissue, followed by diathermy for further dissection. As the procedure progresses closer to the ureter, a harmonic device is employed for distant tissue release, while a bipolar instrument is used for precise tissue separation near the ureter.
The surgeon releases the bladder from the cervix, completing the dissection of the ureter, which is now fully isolated.
